author: @lisakingscott7729
text:
Hi Rob, I know this is an old vid, but I gave this a go in a proper hydrothermal reactor, but as I'd seen elsewhere that adding a small amount of naphthalene can make carbon nanofoam from sugar using HC too, I added some of that too. Same quantities as you, but also less than 0.1g (resolution of my balance) naphthalene. Cooked for similar time and temperature as you (the nanofoam needs longer, but I took your advice and just tried it anyway!!!).

I got the same highly iridescent surface layer. Lovely purple colour in my case and left a brown layer on metal or glass with purple reflections. I didn't bother to do any other tests with it, but just interesting to see as cooking time not too long.

I tried filtering the liquid / solid mix, but most of the particles went straight through my kitchen paper filter. The solid hardly separates after a few hours, when the mixture is left in a beaker, so the particles must be very small. I'll try with a better filter too.

As with my other HC tests, the solid is brown, not black. Any ideas?

As the solid is brown, is it possible it's all GO or some other oxide of carbon variation, rather than carbon? Smell is slightly oily with a hint of naphthalene.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it culd all be GO - the main problem i found with tang lau wasn't the eaction but the purification and characterisation of the products afterwards - I thought it was a bit difficult to do without a truck load of expensive equipment and for chemists starting out with basic equipment it was a bit challeging to get non equivocal results

replies.author: @lisakingscott7729
replies.text:
Many thanks for your reply Rob. An update on the small amount of filtrate left on the filter paper now it's dry. It's a mass of thin film interference colours, mostly greens, pinks and purples. I assume these are GO films. Worth it just to see the colours amongst all the brown matter. I read somewhere that GO has a high RI, which would explain the interference colours I guess.
The carbon nanofoam from Beilstein journal of nanotechnology requires more time, so I'll have another go and leave it longer.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome

replies.author: @lisakingscott7729
replies.text:
Just FYI, totally off the Tang Lau topic, but similar HC process. I reran using a 10x concentrated sugar solution for 5 hours and too much naphthalene (misread g for mg) so the result is a bit smelly!!! Got a lovely very homogeneous light weight carbon foam. Just washing it now. Washed one lump under the tap and dried, just for quick look. Amazingly light weight, consistency like polystyrene, but it's my first completely black carboniferous result from my hydrothermal reactor! Wehey, it does work then ;)
Unfortunately not even slightly conductive, and not obviously magnetic (I've read reports of carbon nanofoams which are diamagnetic). I'm considering trying Nickel chloride as a catalyst to help graphetization, or maybe atempting to graphetize this foam in a microwave, after impregnating with nickel chloride. I'm sure you'll know better ;)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol awesome - funny how often we discover things when we actually get up and do stuff lol

author: @luvbotany
text: I've worked with HTC and sugar. Without catalysts and mixing, you are not gonna get alot of graphene yield. The graphene you are getting is likely the result of the vessel metal catalyizing the reaction which is why your yield is so tiny.  I like this method of making graphene because it's a way of reducing carbon in the atmosphere; grow the plant, harvest the sugar, reduction to graphene by the megaton to produce our high tech world = carbon sequestration.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: all very true mate

replies.author: @luvbotany
replies.text:
By adding carboxylic acid or hydroxides along with plenty of others to HTC, one can catalyze the reaction while also adding functionality of the carboxyl, hydroxyl, ... groups. 
So I was always curious what would happen if I functionalized one side of an ultra cap with carboxyl and across the separator the other side with some opposite hydroxyl. Another application would be metal mining or ocean mining by tuning functional groups to a capacitive deionization water treatment.
Perhaps durability would be an issue but seems like it's worth a try...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is mate - give it a go and let me know how you get on

replies.author: @luvbotany
replies.text: I made some bench top prototypes of various carbon materials and applications, commercialization is a whole nutter story and I moved from the benchtop to greener pastures for me, literally. I'm 100% focused on my current project but still love graphene and imagine the type of applications that it will enable.

author: @bradmiller7001
text:
So, once in a while I get a brain storm that works.   By taking a small conductive wire and then coating it with an insulator and then placing six small Graphene sheets doped to make to be resistive  at differing electrical loads, we have a new surgery tool. 

The heat turns the wire as one side expands and the other does not.  With a similar Graphene tipped point that can be used to destroy cells and tissue in the human body. 

Since we already can make Graphene sheets and dope them after using this creation method and coat very thin wire with an insulator and mount these Graphene sheets to the wire and connect them.    We now have a Nano Surgery Tool that can access all areas of the human body.  The only problem is that you can not push the wire, it must be pulled so the wire must be non-magnetic and the head must contain a small amount of magnetic material that can be drawn by a powerful magnet from outside the body.  

The other method of control would be using the blood stream itself, like a pilot chute used in sky diving.   The wire would be pulled by the blood pressure and maneuvered changing the shape of the vents.  Small heat changes on the Graphene sheets. 

You turn the head of the wire in the vein and then draw down to its target.   So we have two systems of guidance.

Let me outline and make this very simple.  We have a simple nano knife that can access any part of the body via the circulation system that can be computer controlled the same way as a 3 D printer.    My question to you is what would we dope the Graphene with and can it be done with "Graphene Sheets"? 

Since I am in Canada and have publicly published this information it is now under copy right protection and since this has millions of dollars of value - I would like to write a joint paper and apply for a patient for this application with you?   My insight has come from part of your work and therefor you should benefit.

What are your thoughts?

The good news is no more inoperable cancers! ~

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller I think it's an interesting idea mate and you should do it yourself - i appreciate the offer but the work and idea is yours - good luck with it

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller there are lots of things you can dope it with - have a quick look on google scholar that will give you quite a few ideas - i suspect it would be easier to dope it first

replies.author: @leonardbaumgardt
replies.text: Copyright protects your expression of the idea, not the idea itself. In other words: Based on copyright, you can sue others if they copy & paste the text you wrote above. You cannot sue them if they start monetizing your idea. To do that, you'd need a patent.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @bradmiller7001
text:
Teslaphoresis and Graphene -applied electromagnetic fields to self generate the armchair or single zigzag pattern for large production of Graphene Sheets using a modified Tang Lau Method.  

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w1d0Lg6wuvc  

So slowly pull the poles down the shallow flat reaction chamber top to bottom and if the concept is correct - large single layer Graphene sheets from sugar.  Now all I have to do is find some glass baking trays and a way to seal them  together and a way to make a strong magnetic field in an oven.  It is my understanding that heat destroys magnets.  

Now I am off to my local glass shop to get a 8.8 x 11.8 Pyrex container made for the oven, I know their are types of glass that can deal with this pressures.  Drill a small hole and plug it to use as a drain and then  I have a glass that is left with a single layer of Graphene from the oven. 

I don't know how to make an electromagnetic field that would be flat or thin but that would work even better.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller nice comment mate - thank you

author: @bradmiller7001
text:
I have been doing some basic research to find out why I see multiple islands being
formed by this method.   It turns out that we have two patterns (armchair and ZigZag) being formed and these two patterns tend to interfere with each other.   In addition, Graphene Oxide and Graphene show some cis-trans isomerism in each molecule added to the sheet.   

In organic chemistry we get left and right handedness for every thing in nature.   One is
good and the other is bad!    One is made by biological processes and the other is not.  One works in the biological system and the other screws the system up. 

So with most processed sugars you are getting both left handed and right handed
molecules to start with.  It is these patterns that are responsible for the islands of Graphene oxide that are produced by this method. So instead of a single flawless large sheet, we get multiple sheets and flaws due to the orientation of the Graphene Oxide and the armchair and or ZigZag patterns.   Starting with organic sugars would
reduce or even end these problems.(single format not left and right
handed) 

So it might be simple to create a single pattern either Armchair or ZigZag by using a 7 chain sugar called Sedoheptulose from a biological source.  This means this
sugar from the Heptose family has only a left handed orientation and as it is a straight line sugar, the removal of the single oxygen atom should force the resulting Graphene Oxide to form in only one single orientation and pattern.
 This means less flaws in the Graphene Oxide sheets produced in the saturated
solution. 

Flowing Hydrogen over the meniscus layer of the solution at low pressure might even turn the Graphene Oxide into single layer Graphene in a single orientation.  This
is just a guess, who knows? I am not sure if it can be done in the heating phase.   

Heating the water to remove all gases and then bubbling hydrogen gas into the
water just before adding the sugar should also have a very positive effect as the end product should be Graphene rather than Graphene Oxide.  I am not sure of this… 
Using a shallow flat container should have better results than a deep container should also produce better outcomes. 

It  would be interesting to see the results from freezing the finished or the starting solution and then letting it thaw The hydrogen bonding (Ice) should also have an effect on the Armchair/Zigzag orientation using the sugars suggested in your video.
A copper foil at the top of the solution might even help with this, as it defeats the bonding effect of two or more layers from forming.

One of the papers that I read, stated that single layer Graphene is magnetic and with multiple layers this effect disappears.  So in solution we have a way of separating single layer Graphene from multiple layer Graphene. 

Just some thoughts…and grievous errors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller that's really interesting stuff mate - thanks for the post - i am now going to go away and think about this - good one mate

author: @x-menlove2484
text: Talent man

author: @bradmiller7001
text:
As I watched your video, I had some basic questions come to mind, the end product seemed to be formed in the center of the meniscus layer is this layer concave or convex?  

The reason, I am asking is if the liquid is spun at a low regular speed, the product should form at the edges of the Mono Graphene Oxide layer. What I mean is can the layer of MGO be pulled or pushed?  

Your reaction is endothermic, so where the heat is coming from (direction, the center or the external edge), the meniscus layer type and spin should provide clues to producing large flat sheets, that can be drawn off by drawing a tube from the edge of the meniscus layer. - Up or down depending on the meniscus layer type.  

If this is the case, then we have a full production method that can be developed.  I would love to see a video of where the layer is developed in the solution column? Does the Mono Graphene Oxide float up or is it formed at the surface layer at the meniscus layer boundary?  

Can the floating Mono Graphene Oxide layer be manipulated by gravity like pizza dough?

Does the Mono Graphene Oxide grow like a mineral crystal or do the segments just join up this way in solution? 

Would the shape of the container make a difference? a large fat dish  or funnel shape?  

The original solution will have capillary action so where the reaction takes place and how could be manipulated? 

I need to stop now!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller lol - they are all good questions mate. But, the tang lau is a well known method - my first port f call would be the literature and second my own experiments from memory the meniscus is concave the source of heat is obvious mate - it was autoclaved in a steel tube. Tang Lau reports layer by layer top down growth - the rest you are probably going to have to find out for yourself lol

replies.author: @bradmiller7001
replies.text: Plastics provide a convex meniscus where as most metals and glass provide a concave meniscus.   Thank you for the reply.   I have a disability that prevents my active participation but I am not opposed to sponsoring some testing.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller That is a shame mate - I am afraid I don't do outside work for others - I hope you find someone as your ideas are interesting

replies.author: @bradmiller7001
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Tang Lau reports layer by layer top down growth - I was not able to find the layer by layer top down growth quote but if this is correct then we have an answer in my mind for a large single sheet layer. 

A large flat container, large enough to prevent a second atomic layer from forming then we have a single large sheet based upon the dimensions.   So matching the amount of reagents, to the horizontal surface, we can produce a single large layer of one atom thick Graphene sheet as per your example in the video. 

Now all I have to do if find a machinist to make a large flat container that can hold an internal glass plate to lift out and hold the the product.    A vacuum press to drive out the remaining solution leaving an oversized single layer Graphene sheet on the glass or just lift the plate out of solution.

The glass sheet should not be flat but very slightly convex shape, that would allow binding agent to be applied to one side and bind this directly to one of your plastic sheets.  Just spit balling again! 

I over thought this... one my first go around. When I looked at the Graphene in the tube  it looked like multiple layers..   Some days I am smart! Other days I am just very good looking.    

Love your videos...  Cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Brad Miller lol - that was my reading of the paper too - but i am guessing it will form in 'islands' and grow out from there to merge into a covering for the surface. That will mean fracture lines on a single sheet.

Robert Murray-Smith  
Actually, I did not.  I was hoping to get an answer from you.  Where is this elusive paper?  Trying to find it has proven to be quite the time vampire.  The only info I could find, suggested a temp, left an option for lower temps but not a time or time(s) for either..  It is understood the goal is to lower the resistance of the material..  Something to the effect of "The electrical resistivity of the GONs can be tuned by annealing for 8 orders of magnitude ranging from 1000000Ω cm to .01 Ω cm."...  Clearly you are quite handy, I am sure you have a pretty close guess.  Thanks﻿

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Robert Cochran
the original paper is here

 
Bottom-up synthesis of large-scale graphene oxide nanosheets
Libin Tang,ab   Xueming Li,c   Rongbin Ji,b   Kar Seng Teng,d   Guoan Tai,a   Jing Ye,a   Changsong Weia and   Shu Ping Lau*a 
Show Affiliations
J. Mater. Chem., 2012,22, 5676-5683
 
DOI: 10.1039/C2JM15944A

replies.author: @robertcochran6466
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  I was acually looking for an answer to my question about annealing times in a conventional oven (in my kitchen).  Certainly you have tried this.  Is it even possible?  I prefer the KISS method.  Kinda like "Place on cookie sheet, top rack, on BROIL for 1 to 8 days"....  I have access to a pottery kiln if needed, but would like to keep it "in house".  Thanks

author: @geshbeddin
text: just wondering were furnace soot would fall on the scale to graphene I know it is very positively ionized and transparent before the charge is altered by water

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: furnace soot is a complex mixture of fullerenes, nanotubes, amorphous carbons, polyaromatic hydrocarbons along with other structures

author: @Aerros
text:
I found the scientific article about the Bottim-Up synthesis by Tang-Lau's team, but it never stated any info about scaling the autoclave (which is my interest) 

40ml/50ml = 80%
Do you think the 80% capacity model should be replicated for scaling purposes? I just want to know your opinion. thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: scaling will most likely be defined by a linear relation - the best bet is just to experiment to estimate the scaling factor

author: @m3sca1
text:
I had a crack at this method last night-obviously did not tighten my pipe fittings enough.Could only get to 150 degrees Celcius with the cooker i was using.
I had 3 vessels in total and only 1 had water left in it when i opened them.
After rinsing the dry vessels and the wet one,and keeping the wash,i can see the same oily looking reflective floaters in places on the top.
Looks like it made some graphene.
Also just tried cooking on the stove top with vinegar and sugar,in a pot with a lid.
Same floater showing but not much of it.

Thinking a pressure cooker would be a good thing for this process.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sounds like a good plan - try giving it longer

author: @aleph0540
text: Indeed, I am a student.  CorboSol is VERY new, and Argonne is interested in Li oxide infused carbon batteries as well.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hi, my e-mail is robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com - feel free to write - i warn you the book  is a more of a how to do it guide as opposed to an in depth study of graphene and the various production methods. I thought Argonne were mostly into advanced Li-ion? Never heard of CorboSol - so I guess you are very new. You don't seem to have done much in this field and you seem to have a theoretical background but i wish you luck - Rob.

author: @aleph0540
text: To continue, I wish to talk more in depth about learning more about this and understanding the advanced chemistry behind it.  I will begin reading the book you've posted, but contact me at oluwamide@uchicago.edu.

author: @aleph0540
text: I work with CorboSol LLC, a group of students who have formed a company at the University of Chicago courting Argonne's licencing favor for their battery tech research (they are opening up a local office on campus for recruitment and research purposes).  We have a lot of interesting ideas from our various disciplines and we want to integrate them and flesh them out together using existing tech and research.  One such concept is the Lang-Tau process.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: definitely - i'd go with the tour method though if i were you

author: @Strangleemall
text: So if I were to create a thicker GO solution by leaving it in the oven longer, would I be able to sonicate it for use in the light-scribe process?  I am waiting on parts for my equipment, and I am going to be using the improved method outlined by Dr. Jame Tour.  If the method you show in this video can be utilized to create a suitable GO solution, then I can begin quite a bit sooner than I thought.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: the preference of sheet growth is just a self organizing property of a lot of things in nature. that is why crystals form, why you can grow your own crystals. It's the same thing as emergent behavior in chaotic systems.

author: @grehuy
text:
That's not an answer ;-) ... the preference of a sheet growth compared to building new sheets. Why does this happen? Why is the seed so "attractive"? 
Do you have an idea? - Anyone?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I can tell you are a fan - each to their own

author: @grehuy
text:
Don't quite understand, Robert, why you are not excited about this route. In general HTC is such a "cloudy" thing, you must be excited about, but nobody really understands because it is so much "statistical". But what the GO synthesis shows here, is close to crystalization out of a melt.
What is really impressive, is the preference of a sheet growth compared to building new sheets. Why does this happen? Why is the seed so "attractive"?

author: @thagrit
text: have you seen tic tac supercapacitor yet?  It's here on youtube.  I am interested in your take on it and would like to know what would be the best binder for making cheap supercapacitors?  Thanks

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text:
Sorry for the late reply, I've been hectic as of late and didn't get an email from YouTube with your reply. But yes I did see a post on the subject a few weeks back showing GO accelorates oxidation while r-GO resists corrosion, and now asking around different circles for the reference and searching high and low for it. I will not let this rest until I do. Also, check this out: 
iopscience.iop.org/0957-4484/24/21/215604  I know you will like this but the paper isn't free but well worth it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I haven't read that paper do you have the reference?

author: @jonathanluxient3002
text: ...the hydrophilic side does the oxidising

author: @jonathanluxient3002
text: would account for the recent paper on how copper oxidises much faster with a coating of graphene.

author: @jonathanluxient3002
text: Yes, that would help adhere the GO ..i can see that in my head with the epoxy groups on the plane

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's interesting how one idea always sparks off another.. The frosted glass effect has set me thinking. I know that the growth mechanism is from the top down my guess is the edge groups attach as normal but the centre plane groups only attach to one side. It's a guess. But vey interesting stuff

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text: And, by my application of the few layers of graphene on the frosted glass, it optically hid the etching and looks like I had it perfectly polished. I left the hydrophobic side out, and the stuff works better than anything I came across to repel water on glass and reduces fogging on the surface brilliantly :)) it is ultra transparent, being only 2 layers thick. It is *impressive* how clear it made the frosted glass!!

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text: Wow, while coating a finely frosted piece of glass with monolayers of pristine graphene, just a moment ago, I noticed that with each alternating layer I applied (after drying) would want to be inverted and made it rather tricky to transfer by submerging with glass under the meniscus. Clearly, one side is hydrophilic, and the other, is hydrophobic. This became quite apparent while manually adding each layer singlehandedly. What causes this?? Is there a theory on this? No model explains this.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Very very cool

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text:
It works wonderfully!! I'm happy now :))))

I dumped the contents into a bath and figured how to heal the bits I made into large flakes some measuring around 3"x4".. now to transfer it into another containter for the cleaning stage. Then off to be reduced/doped. Finally, I have flakes big enough to play with.

replies.author: @michealdumasii5432
replies.text: Can you explain how you figured out how to make bigger flakes? Also do you have a microscopy to see any lattice defects?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text: Baking some now at 168C after sorting the autoclave from 3/4" steel pipe fittings.. anxiously awaits!! Got just under 50m to go. :) wish I could﻿ see inside of it to know when to stop.

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text:
Interesting!  Of all the months searching for another method to make graphene, I get a call on my mobile at 1am telling me they saw a video on making graphene out of ordinary table sugar.. I drove straight home and jumped on and found it still on the phone. You made my day sir!

I actually was totally unaware of the Tang Lau method (Libin Tang et al) and been up to now been struggling with wanting a CVD reactor/oven. If this can yield large-area pristine graphene, I will be most happy indeed. :)

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lol good luck mate sincerely

author: @JK360noscope
text:
Ok, so after a few weeks, I did finally purchase a nice 50 mL autoclave, the same type in the original paper. I have a nice convection oven at home and did a test experiment. 
The results initially looked disappointing. Upon further inspection, I found very small particles on the surface of the water, only becoming seen when a glare covered the surface of the water.

I will be increasing temperature and time in the oven, wish me luck!

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: They are very different. that's a bit like asking the difference between copper oxide and copper - as you know they are very different. The quality of difference is a little more subtle but the idea is the same. I do indeed know how to make a membrane. The technique is all over the net - read around i'm sure you will get it - good luck with your project.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Honestly, I don't know. You might have problems with burning spots out, not reducing spots and tracing out any intricate paths you might have. time you leave the light spot in one place and distance from lens to surface are obvious control factors as is overlapping. But you don't learn anything if you don't try it out - so good luck with it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lightscribed reduced GO is pretty good it does need charging - use a charging circuit the same as you would any capacitor but at much lower voltage. I'm not sure of the power density i'm afraid and it would be intimately affected by the construction quality you manange. Thanks for the kind comments

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hiya mate, I'm not a fan of this method. Too many problems and not enough yield. My own favourites are the electrochemical method and the chemical method. Give them a go you will be happier with the results - thanks for grabbing the book and lots of luck

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I can't remember exactly what the temperature limit is on the tape but I had it at 200 for 16 hours in one experiment and it did just fine

author: @caldasReport
text:
Greetings. I've already tried twice and it hasn't worked in the autoclave. The one we have at school doesn't get hot enough. The first time I tried using the tang-lau method, I didn't tape it properly so it leaked. The second batch is in the oven right now and it's not leaking. Hope it works! I purchased your book, btw. Great great job.
Thanks, man!

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: that's ok - you need to apply the tape properly or it willl leak and you will have to start again. It's not challenging but you must do it carefully. I don't know much about the pipe you have there so check the pressure ratings before you do it. If you are worried do it outside and surround the oven with sandbags just in case. It should be ok though - but as it is a school project better safe than sorry.

author: @caldasReport
text:
Hello Robert. Great method!! How does the autoclave fare in the oven? Is the pipe nipple really important? I'm interested in recreating thin at school here in Colombia and I'd like to know how the tape does at 200°C. How does the autoclave behave when  heated for 3 hours? And also, mine is made out of galvanized iron. Would long exposure to heat damage it in any way? Please excuse all these questions. I'm very interested in trying this!!
Thank you.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It forms at the air liquid interface first then grows down from there. If you have a Teflon lined autoclave you will get much better results. Thickness of layer is moderated by time temperature and precursor concentrations. I would be very interested in your results. Let me know when you have posted and all the best.

author: @JK360noscope
text:
Hello,

I am a student at University of North Carolina at Charlotte, and am very interested in high quality graphene synthesis (as many are). 

I have access to several very high temperature ovens (used for high purity silicon oxidations), but am confused about where the graphene layer forms using the Tang-Lau Method:
 Does the graphene layer form on the top of the solution, or does it form on the parts where the hot metal fitting contacts the solution?

I will be posting any progress I make.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I agree it does appear very similar to the htc of sugar. It is not though - hence the missing acid. I think it could probably work very well under microwave conditions. Give it a go and let me know what happens.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: it was more than a layer thick - i made sure it was so you could see it. About the only way of knowing you have anything if you make it thinner is to anneal it and measure resistivity. if the resistance is infinite - you haven't got anything. I went through how to control thickness on the vid.

author: @mrvos96
text:
Maybe this is a stupid question, but how do you know that it is a single layer of graphene?   If it was a single layer, it would be invisible or is a bit of solution stuck under it, wich makes it visible? How come we saw it when it was in the glass, if it was a single layer, it would be too tin to be visible. Also, is there any method of finding out the thickness? 
Your videos are great
Regards, mrvos

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: for super capacitors reduced graphene oxide is best as the surface area is large. If you use other forms of graphene you will have to activate them. The other forms are good for transparent conductive fils and inks

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I wish. It's mostly water impurities forming insoluble inorganic salt complexes.
